text:
Unexpected 5xx elevation from ingress priority debounce and prefetch livelock during tenant weight rebalancing

Issue summary: Customers (Verity Labs) observed a sustained increase in 5xx responses on chat and embedding routes during a tenant weight rebalance window. Impact: multiple requests returned 502/503/500 across short lived bursts, causing degraded application throughput and user-visible failures. Timeline: 2026-03-10 19:04 UTC — first error spike detected by customer dashboards; 19:10 UTC — automated alert from apigw 5xx-rate > 1%; 19:20 UTC — customer opened support case. Observed behavior: 5xx spikes correlate with ingress priority rebalancer runs and high prefetch cancellation rates; runtime pods report kvcache lock contention and prefetch worker saturation. This ticket captures the customer-facing incident, investigation artifacts, and mitigation steps.
1) Authenticate as the tenant and send mixed traffic: 70% small chat requests with priority header X-Tenant-Priority: high, 30% bulk embedding batches. 2) Trigger a weight rebalancing by toggling route weights or deploying a small canary change (simulated via traffic-shift API). 3) Observe for ~2-10 minutes: apigw 5xx-rate increases, runtime logs show prefetch cancellations and kvcache acquire timeouts. Example curl (repro): curl -X POST https://api.redwood.example/v1/chat -H 'Authorization: Bearer <key>' -H 'X-Tenant-Priority: high' -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hello"}]}'
- apigw: 5xx_rate: baseline 0.02% -> spike 3.7% at 2026-03-10T19:09Z
- ingress-prioritizer: rebalancer run at 2026-03-10T19:08:45Z, convergence time 18s (normal 2-3s)
- serving-runtime: repeated 'kvcache: lock_acquire_timeout=1200ms' and 'prefetch: cancel_on_priority_shift' warnings
- pod restarts: none, but threads starved on runtime process; cpu steal spike noted on host
- example log snippets: 
  [2026-03-10T19:09:03Z] apigw: upstream returned 502 for tenant=verity-labs route=/v1/chat request_id=abc123
  [2026-03-10T19:09:04Z] runtime: prefetch cancel_on_priority_shift request=abc123 reason=priority-window-adjust
  [2026-03-10T19:09:05Z] runtime: kvcache lock_acquire_timeout shard=42 duration_ms=1250

Ingress prioritizer's debounce window and the weight-rebalance algorithm are interacting under mixed small-payload chat + embedding upload workloads. Rapid changes cause a flurry of priority state updates; prefetch workers receive cancellation signals, requeue work, and attempt to reacquire KV cache locks, producing effective livelock and throughput collapse which surfaces as 5xx at the gateway due to timeouts and hedged-retries.
19:25 UTC - Correlated rebalancer timestamps from ingress logs with apigw spike window; multiple rebalancer runs within 30s observed when weight convergence was slow.
19:40 UTC - Heap and thread dump from runtime captured; threads waiting on kvcache mutexes with exponential backoff. No OOMs or GPU driver resets detected.
20:05 UTC - SRE simulated identical traffic mix in staging and reproduced increased kv lock contention when rebalancer run frequency set to <30s.
20:30 UTC - Noted that prefetch workers immediately cancel older prefetches on priority change and resubmit; queue growth observed in runtime metrics (prefetch_queue_size).
Support (Aisha): Customer reported 5xx spike impacting live users; provided initial logs and example request IDs. Asked for priority escalations due to SLA.
SRE (Liam): Attached timeline correlating ingress rebalancer and apigw spike. Requested runtime thread dumps and kvcache metrics. Assigned to runtime oncall.
Runtime Oncall (Mei Chen): Reproduced prefetched cancellation loop in staging with stress tool. Proposed short-term mitigation: increase ingress debounce window and add backoff to prefetch resubmission.
Customer (Verity Labs): Confirmed they are seeing a drop in successful responses; asked if we can apply hotfix in region while investigation continues.
Applied emergency config: increase ingress-prioritizer debounce window from 1s -> 5s for us-east-1 at 2026-03-11T01:12Z (rollout to affected edge nodes). Added 200ms randomized jitter to prefetch resubmission to reduce synchronized thrashing. Result: apigw 5xx_rate dropped from 3.7% -> 0.12% within 6 minutes.
An interaction between ingress priority debounce settings and the prefetch cancellation/resubmit path under mixed traffic caused synchronized work cancellation and requeueing. This produced kvcache lock contention (livelock-like behavior) which increased tail latency and triggered upstream timeouts at the gateway, surfacing as 5xx errors.
1) runtime: implement prefetch exponential backoff + cap on immediate resubmits (PR #7242).
2) ingress: add safety to weight-rebalancer to avoid consecutive runs within a short window (configurable min-interval).
3) kvcache: make lock acquisition non-blocking with a best-effort fallback path for low-priority prefetches.
4) e2e: add regression test that simulates mixed embed/chat workloads during weight rebalances in CI to prevent regressions.
Hot-config rollback not required. Emergency debounce increase and jitter reduced customer impact. Runtime PR (#7242) opened to add controlled backoff and cap on prefetch resubmissions. Planning an orchestrated rollout across regions with monitoring playbook. Customer notified and satisfied with the mitigation; engineering will follow up with a patch and regression tests.
TRACK-981: runtime PR review and canary rollout to prod (owner: Mei Chen) - target 2026-03-18
OPS-542: ingress rebalancer config change as permanent flag with gradual rollout (owner: SRE team) - target 2026-03-16
DOC-77: update runbook and postmortem for prefetch livelock scenarios (owner: oncall) - target 2026-03-20
2026-03-11 02:00 UTC - Notified Verity Labs that we applied an emergency config tweak (ingress debounce + prefetch jitter) and are tracking improvement. 2026-03-12 15:00 UTC - Sent follow-up with planned permanent fix timeline and invite to technical postmortem.

title: Embeddings rotational-dimension mismatch causing rerank score entropy and long queueing
summary: Customer reports sudden increase in reranker score entropy (low top-k stability) and long request queueing when sending precomputed embeddings to the /v1/rerank endpoint. Impact: Search relevance degraded for ~40% of queries; median tail latency increased from ~120ms to ~900ms with periodic spikes to >5s. No new model rollouts on our side. Environment: LexaSearch uses enterprise account with pinned model 'reranker-v2' and uploads externally computed 384-d embeddings under a legacy schema. Observ
text:
Embeddings rotational-dimension mismatch causing rerank score entropy and long queueing

Issue summary: Customer reports sudden increase in reranker score entropy (low top-k stability) and long request queueing when sending precomputed embeddings to the /v1/rerank endpoint. Impact: Search relevance degraded for ~40% of queries; median tail latency increased from ~120ms to ~900ms with periodic spikes to >5s. No new model rollouts on our side. Environment: LexaSearch uses enterprise account with pinned model 'reranker-v2' and uploads externally computed 384-d embeddings under a legacy schema. Observed around 2026-03-01 09:15 PDT.
1) Customer constructs embeddings via their internal pipeline (reported shape header 1x384, binary proto payload).
2) POST to /v1/rerank with reranker-v2 and include 'embed-dim: 384' header and 'normalize: true' flag in request metadata.
3) Observe response: successful 200 but rerank order fluctuates significantly across repeated identical requests; backend logs show batch queuing and CPU fallback events.
4) Re-run with Redwood-generated embeddings (text-embed-large-4096 -> projection to 384 via our client helper) and observe stable ranks and normal latency.
Sample API payload header: Content-Length: 4820; embed-dim: 384; encoding: proto; customer-id: lexa-42
Sample server trace (excerpt): serving-runtime: batch enqueue delay=412ms; kernel-select=quantized-path; fallback=cpu at 2026-03-01T09:16:01Z
Rerank response example (repeated requests show different order despite identical inputs): [docA:0.72, docB:0.69, docC:0.68] -> [docB:0.73, docA:0.71, docC:0.67]
Observed resource logs: gpu-mem-high events on node gpu-7 followed by OOM-eviction and request requeueing to CPU worker pool.
Customer notes that the issue started after they rolled a light-weight dimension projection step in their embedding pipeline to reduce storage. They believe their projection preserves cosine relationships but suspect a change in basis orthogonality. Customer seeing both quality regressions (top-k instability) and latency regressions (long queueing).
Support performed quick validation: reproduced non-deterministic rerank order using customer's serialized embeddings. No model config change detected on Redwood side. We observed mismatch between declared embed-dim header and actual vector byte-length in some payloads (some messages included stray 8-byte metadata footer). Also seen: when vectors exceed expected memory footprint for batch, serving runtime spilled to CPU leading to long tail latencies.
Hypothesis A: Embeddings are using a rotated/orthonormal basis different from Redwood's canonical normalization which, combined with our reranker projection codepath, yields numerical sensitivity and nondeterministic top-k due to floating point ties.
Hypothesis B: Malformed payloads (extra footer bytes) cause our batch size estimator to undercount vectors leading to oversized batches on GPU and OOM spill to CPU.
Checked: ingestion logs for customer show ~7% of POSTs with byte_length not dividing cleanly by 384*4 (float32), indicating occasional serialization mismatch.
Reproduced locally by injecting 8 trailing bytes into proto payload: triggers batch oversize and CPU fallback; rerank scores became unstable due to fallback ordering differences across replicas.
1) Ask customer to stop using the lightweight projection and send Redwood-produced embeddings until full fix in place. 2) If projection must be used, require strict SFH (schema) validation on client side to ensure no extra footer bytes; temporarily set per-request header 'force-normalize=true' to force our server side normalization code (may increase compute).
1) Eng to add stricter payload validation and early-reject malformed embedding sizes with clear 400 error and helpful message. 2) Runtime team to make CPU fallback deterministic by preserving original batch ordering before fallback to reduce nondeterminism. 3) Work with customer to audit projection implementation and provide example serializer compatible with Redwood APIs.
Liam O'Neill: coordinate on reproducer and collect sample toxic payloads from customer (due 2026-03-04)
SRE (Anika Patel): investigate GPU OOM pattern and add backpressure metric for embed byte mismatch (due 2026-03-06)
Runtime team (PR queued): add early validation hook for embedding size header and return 400 with details (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/service/pull/1247)
DX (Samir Rao): provide customer with a portable serializer snippet for float32 embeddings and a small test harness.
(ongoing) Temporary mitigation implemented: support added server-side to detect and reject payloads where byte-length != embed-dim * 4 * n_entries with a clear error. Customer applied serializer fix and sent a validation run; quality and latency returned to baseline for validated requests. Final fixes include runtime determinism improvements and enhanced SDK guidance.
Mitigation applied; awaiting runtime determinism patch and customer verification across traffic spike window.
2026-03-01 Maya Chen (Support): Received from LexaSearch: "We started noticing weird search order flips yesterday and longer waits for results. Nothing else changed on our end except a small projection step to reduce vector size."
2026-03-01 Liam O'Neill (Eng): I reproduced locally using injected footer bytes. Directory reproduction steps noted. Logging shows GPU spill followed by CPU rerank; will open a PR for validation and deterministic fallback behavior.
2026-03-02 Anika Patel (SRE): Added temporary alert for gpu-oom-eviction spikes and backpressure. Observed two nodes showing repeated spills correlated with customer tenant id. Increasing reserved pool for that tenant as immediate relief.
2026-03-03 LexaSearch (Customer): Confirmed that after running our provided serializer test, the instability and latency disappeared for the test batch. Will run canary on 10% of traffic tomorrow.
2026-03-05 Liam O'Neill (Eng): PR #1247 opened for payload validation; follow-on PR planned to make fallback ordering preserved during CPU spill. Marking in-progress until PRs are merged and customer can run production canary.

title: Staggered warmup resonance reduces reserved GPU attach efficiency and causes gradual throughput erosion
summary: Customer ScribeAI reports a gradual drop in throughput (10-30% over ~90 minutes) on their dedicated reserved pool. The pattern is periodic: brief partial recoveries every ~8–12 minutes followed by renewed p99 and p50 regression. Customer is on dedicated capacity in us-east-1 and started a multi-region campaign that created many short-lived prewarm connections from several services concurrently.
text:
Staggered warmup resonance reduces reserved GPU attach efficiency and causes gradual throughput erosion

Issue summary:
Customer ScribeAI reports a gradual drop in throughput (10-30% over ~90 minutes) on their dedicated reserved pool. The pattern is periodic: brief partial recoveries every ~8–12 minutes followed by renewed p99 and p50 regression. Customer is on dedicated capacity in us-east-1 and started a multi-region campaign that created many short-lived prewarm connections from several services concurrently.

Impact:
- Production embeddings and chat routes served from customer reserved pool show sustained p99 latency increase from ~180ms to ~420ms and p50 drift from ~60ms to ~150ms.
- Overall token throughput reduced by ~22% during observed window.
- A subset of requests returns transient 500s during highest peaks due to allocator attach delays.

Environment:
- Dedicated reserved pool (customer-owned reservation) in us-east-1
- Serving runtime version: v2.13.1
- Autoscaler image: autoscale-v1.4.0
- Network: VPC peering with customer

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Start N=300 short-lived warmup sessions that perform model chunking and persistent keepalives, staggered by random offsets across 10 services.
2) After 30 minutes, start burst of short inference streams (avg 200 tokens) across same pool.
3) Observe allocator attach latency and periodic throughput erosion over next ~90 minutes.

Observed behavior:
- Autoscaler oscillates: small upscales then downscales within minutes leading to capacity mismatch.
- Allocator logs show attach latency spikes and repeated prefetch attempts for same KV shards.
- GPU attach failures surface as transient 5xx for ~0.5% of requests at peak.
- Metric pattern looks like a resonant waveform rather than a single spike, suggesting cohort alignment of warmups.

Customer impact comment:
"This is throttling our real-time chat traffic and increases our costs due to autoscale churn. We need a mitigation while you investigate."
2026-03-13 10:12 - Support (Aisha Patel): Acknowledged. Triage and assigned to Infra Eng. Requested telemetry (alloc logs, autoscaler traces, and p99 timelines). SLA first response due 2026-03-14.
2026-03-13 11:03 - Customer (ScribeAI - L. Nguyen): Sent sample load generator script and pan-Regional IP ranges used for warmup. Noted that the problem coincided with a deploy that increased parallel prewarmers.
2026-03-13 13:20 - Eng (Miguel Ortega): Observed resonant waveform on pool attach-latency and allocator queue depth. NVLink/PCIe counters fine; kernel logs show frequent context-switches from sidecars during warmup windows.
2026-03-13 15:45 - Eng (SRE - Priya Rao): Applied temporary routing to fallback standby shards to reduce attach competition. Short-term p99 improved by ~18%.
2026-03-14 09:02 - Customer: Confirmed improvement but reports periodic regressions persist during large warmup bursts. Asked for recommended client-side throttling.
2026-03-14 12:30 - Eng (Miguel Ortega): Drafted rolling autoscaler smoothing patch and a guidance doc for staggered warmup best-practices. Planning canary deploy to us-east-1 tomorrow.
2026-03-14 16:00 - Support (Aisha Patel): Notified customer of next steps and timeline for canary.
Checked allocator histogram: attach latency p99 correlates with number of concurrent prefetch jobs and number of distinct small connections; heatmap shows clusters every ~9 minutes.
Autoscaler logs: scale decisions happen on short windows (30s) and see several up/down cycles; smoothing window currently 2m - too small to damp cohort-driven oscillation.
Prefetcher behavior: repeated prefetch for overlapping KV ranges when many short connections each request cold prefixes; leads to redundant cache thrash and GPU memory churn.
Kernel/driver logs: no GPU thermal throttling; few driver-level stalls; main contention appears at allocator and sidecar scheduling layer rather than GPU hardware.
Network: VPC peering healthy; transient TLS handshake bursts observed consistent with many simultaneous short-lived sessions.
Hypothesis: Staggered warmup cohorts across several services produced a resonant load pattern which the autoscaler misinterprets as demand spikes, causing frequent up/down actions that increase attach contention and reduce effective throughput.
Applied short-term mitigations: (1) route a portion (20%) of traffic to standby fallback shards to absorb attach peaks, (2) asked customer to reduce parallel prewarmers by 50% and increase keepalive duration for warm connections, (3) temporarily increased autoscaler smoothing to 5m for pool to reduce oscillation.
- Stagger warmup starts deterministically (e.g., jitter > 2m) across services.
- Prefer persistent connections rather than many short-lived prewarmers.
- If using client-side prefetch, add a cooperative backoff when attach latency exceeds 200ms.
1) Canary deploy autoscaler smoothing patch (PR linked) in us-east-1 and monitor for 24h. 2) Implement allocator-level de-duplication for overlapping KV prefetches (engineering ticket). 3) Follow up with customer after canary with recommended config changes and re-run load test. 4) If canary successful, rollout to other regions.
Pending - temporary routing improved p99 by ~18% but full resolution requires autoscaler smoothing patch and allocator dedupe. Engineering to own rollout.
